title: High asymmetry and dependent-spouse caution without premature severe escalation
scenario_summary: One divorced parent has historically handled nearly all expense paperwork, reimbursements, and account access. The other parent is less informed, less confident, and worried about agreeing blindly. The session remains workable, but Solomon should protect informed participation and stay in bounded caution rather than forcing commitment or jumping immediately to a severe posture.
intended_challenge_type: Fair-process protection and asymmetry-sensitive caution without premature high-severity escalation.

public_goals: Understand the real expense picture before agreeing to reimbursement expectations.
Avoid saying yes just because the paperwork and accounts have always been controlled by the other parent.
Get a fair next step that improves clarity without another escalation.
starting_positions: Primary position: I need to see and understand the records before any reimbursement expectation is treated as fair.
Secondary position: a shared written summary process would be safer than trying to settle the numbers verbally.

public_goals: Get the reimbursement discussion moving without having to re-explain every record from the beginning.
Avoid being treated as if handling the paperwork means acting in bad faith.
Agree on a clearer document-sharing and summary process if that is what is needed.
interest_profile: A shared records process that stops repeated confusion
starting_positions: Primary position: the records can be shared, but the conversation needs more structure so it does not turn into the same confusion repeatedly.
Secondary position: written summaries and a shared review step are better than forcing reimbursement conclusions now.
